3 - How Went from 0 to 100kMonth in 3 Months Back to 0 with Nick Metzger.mp4 All right. All right. Thank you, Jeremy, for that introduction. And thank you. I was so fucking honored when you actually texted me, 'Hey, want to speak at the Mastermind?' I was like, 'Fucking yeah.' I'm going to do that. I'm going to speak in front of a room of ballers. So basically, what I'm going to talk about today is how we went from zero to 100k a month in three months with our new consulting offer. And then back to zero the next. That's the full story. All right. So you're going to get some baller lessons that we learned from that. Simple lessons, but I think the simpler the better. The easier it gets, the more familiar it becomes. But I'm gonna get you my backstory first. So some of you know it, some of you don't. I practice parkour for like seven years. Before hmm. That's Mia, yeah. Before I had a tumor in my back that actually just stopped my end of my career in that space because I really wanted to become a stuntman, like I was so focused on going to Hollywood, like becoming a stuntman, working on movies. So I practice every day, like two to three hours every day, seven days a week. Then the tumor happened and I couldn't train anymore. Parkour is like one of these sports— one of these things that you do that trains you mentally, like it makes you so freaking strong mentally. Because, at first, this might look lame to some, might look cool to some, whatnot. I think everybody knows what a backflip is, right? Okay, so how do you learn a backflip? Shout it out. I just started jumping and spinning. Well, go for it. Exactly. You find someone that knows how. This guy actually shows it to you first, then you film it, then you look at the video in slow motion, then you try to copy that movement into like a foam pit, right? Then as soon as you feel comfortable, comfortable in a foam pit, you start doing it on a mat. As soon as it feels comfortable on that mat, you do it on a little mat. As soon as you feel comfortable on that one, you do it on the floor, on the grass, and then on concrete. So it's like a step-by-step process, and it's pretty much like building a business. So that prepared me for actually building up a business afterwards. Okay. And this screen grab right here is a video that we did that was actually our first paid video. We charged 4K for it, which at that time was... To me. Like 4,000 bucks for one single video was crazy. In the meanwhile, like right now, we would charge about 35k for such a video just to give you a little bit of perspective. But because I did parkour and I had this tumor in my back, right? I was kind of forced to do something else. Like, I couldn't just stop crying and sit in the corner and lose all my friends. No, I just filmed videos for my friends doing parkour. And then that actually led to the dad of a friend of mine saying, 'Hey, Nick, I've seen all your videos. I'm like the chief of the fire department. Do you want to film a video for me?' I did that. It has, as you can see, 700,000 views on YouTube and gave them like 20 new applicants for their fire department, and they were searching for five. So at that time, I was like, 'Fuck.' I made a shit ton of money. Shit ton back then. Shit ton of money. Had fun doing it and they solved a problem. That's what it's all about. That's what Jeremy teaches all the time. You got to solve a fucking problem and you get paid well. I found it Venturesome. So Venturesome is my video production company. Which I'm actually in the process of selling right now, after talking to you, like, how many times? Probably 10, 12 times— about should I sell it, should I not sell it? I finally decided... It's not going to be a huge lump sum. It's going to be about 300k—I can sell it for. But that will happen in the next two to three months. I realized that we did videos for big corporates, we did videos for smaller sized businesses, but all of them had one similar problem. What do you guys think was the problem when we produced the video for them? That had distribution problems. That's it. That's it. They had no fucking idea how to distribute it. And out of that, I pretty much, because of that issue that we had, I bought the DMM. And that's how I learned about Jeremy. Yeah, got your ad in the right time so pretty much learned about that and then found the money tree which at the beginning was a marketing agency. Right so I thought, if we can produce the video and we do have a marketing agency on top of it, we can then distribute it all for the client. So we did that. And we were working with over 25 industries, which was huge and chaotic because, like, we had account managers that could handle about seven clients max. Now, to all of you guys that have ads running for clients, how many clients can one account manager handle? 100. What else? Yeah, right. What about you guys? What was the question again? How many clients can you handle as one account manager? About ten. Ten? Yeah. Okay. Any other numbers? Fifteen. Fifteen? Cool. So everybody's in that range, except for Julian. He cracked the code. You've got to think of something, my friend. Yeah, right? Thank you. Yeah, but it was the exact same thought process for me. We scaled up to 12 people in the team. Pretty much fucked it up because I had a guarantee. Like I pulled off what Jeremy preaches. I said, 'Hey, we're going to get you at least double the amount you invest in us within the first month and we pay you back.' I had to refund about 80k in revenue, but I had like 80k in overhead already due to the team, Switzerland is fucking expensive, staff is more expensive. Went to zero with my entire cash flow and my cash reserve. Which at that time was chaotic. I was glad I had Jeremy. That I could go into the calls and ask for advice because I was pretty fucked up sometimes. And that was the time when I. Maybe if I want to scale, I cannot work with everybody, I cannot take just every single deal, but rather focus on one niche, which were car dealerships. So we focused on getting leads to car dealerships. That was actually after our call Send, after you told me, 'Hey, Nick, you've got to focus on one thing.' I did that. went after car dealerships, but the problem with these car dealerships was we generated an average of 70 leads per month for them with an ad spend of like 500 to 700 bucks. What the hell? Which in Switzerland is a pretty good number. But we have one problem. What do you guys think it was? Yes, exactly. If they even closed. And you know why? Dude, these closers, they should go work at McDonald's because they are just fucking order takers. They can sell a car if somebody comes in and says, 'I want that Toyota Prius.' Can I get it right now please? Then they can sell it. But as soon as they only have like an offer, like let's say, Zero money down leasing, an example, and the client doesn't know what car they want yet, they just come in and they look for that zero money down leasing. Then the problem is the closer. Dude, that guy is just lost. So I thought, well, I have Jeremy. I can get someone else who is focusing on pretty much this kind of business, like scaling an ad agency with, like, smaller ticket clients. So I had this fifty thousand dollar fix which I invested fifty thousand dollars into these guys who know them. You've seen them before? They're huge. These are called the brothers. They built a consulting business in Germany from zero to 25 million a year. Three years or something. Pretty crazy. They have 40 employees. And I thought, okay, I'm just going to pretty much hire them to help me build that agency. Out of that was pretty much that I realized, hey, wait a second. If they can build a consulting offer from zero to 25 million in Like three years, I bet I can do that. Like. From zero to one million, right? And I was pretty much prey of the shiny object syndrome in that scenario because I thought, 'Wow, these guys are killing it. These guys are smashing it. It seems so much easier than the marketing agency. Everything is so much better. Life is great. I'm going to do the same. Okay. So, Since I had the video agency already, I built like a video training with over 150 videos within like two weeks. Everything, shot everything, edited everything, uploaded it to Kajabi, made it ready and in that time frame already started Selling people on it. And I said to myself, okay, That was actually in May this year, last year, so May 2021. And I said to myself, okay, cool. We're going to reach 100K a month by the end of the year. That was my goal. Hopped on a call with Jeremy, told him about it. What do you guys think he said? That's what he said. What do you need to do? To achieve it by September, motherfucker. All right. Who's going to do it? Okay, San's going to do it. Perfect. Jeremy's NFT dope, okay. So I had the goal to go to get to 100K a month. Um, was super excited about it. Jeremy said, okay. What do you need to do to achieve it by September? Obviously, what did I need to do first? Take a go. He knows what's up. All right, so I did the math. You can do that. Yeah, so I did the math, which was pretty basic. I could do like 13 8k deals paid up front for that consulting. And I'm going to tell you a A little bit about the consulting offer afterwards, like how we structure it, how we deliver it. I could do 13 deals and have some change left over. I could do 100 1k month deals. 6 times 8k up front plus 52 1k a month, or I could do like two 36k deals and four 8k deals up front, etc. etc. Right there are so many options to it. And just calculate it through everything. But what stuck to me was okay, wait this one right here like 13 8k deals. That's not hard. Everybody can close like 13 clients a month, right? So I did that. By using what I call the perpetual mobility strategy, and this might not be something that you see the first time, but it's our spin on that. So pretty much what it is is: first of all, define the target audience. Right, because you got some somebody to to sell your offer to. Then you define the product. Define an offer, define the pricing, define everything that's needed to actually sell that audience. Then you've got to get leads, okay? Otherwise, well, you're fucked. Then you close the deal. Then you deliver on it. You get reviews and referrals. And from there on, it's a perpetual mobile. So you just push it once in the beginning, and then it just starts spinning and spinning and spinning and spinning and never stops. Okay. Now, how did we actually pull that off? These are going to be the things that you want to take notes on. Defining the target audience for us in the beginning, we didn't have one. Because I worked with that model without even knowing I worked with that model and I forgot the two upper parts. I started with getting leads, closing deals, delivering. And getting reviews, but I never even defined an exact target audience. I just said service-based businesses. That was always a big problem of mine. To actually niche down and say, okay, we're only going to work with these types of people. We did that later, which I'm going to make the example of now. So right now, today, we help videographers who make less than 25k a month. That's our audience. To find the product. So giving your clients desperately what they want means using this framework. I won't dream in time without obstacle. So for videographers, that means I want to make $25,000 plus a month in 90 days without obstacle. Without giving up shooting videos. Because these guys are artists. So now, secondly... obviously ads, right? We did Facebook ads and we had like a free course offer, so we identified that the biggest problem for most service providers was that they didn't know how to the deal. We got that out of the car dealerships; they knew how to get leads with us, but they didn't know how to close the deal. So we made a short, little video training about how you can actually close deals, how you can actually close more objections, and how you can handle objections. It was like a training of about 15– 16 videos. And then pretty much, we put that offer up, and then in that training, we did it in Kajabi. Everybody who uses Kajabi, like on the right hand side, you can actually put little banners or posts, what they call it. And we put our Facebook group there. Because we had a free Facebook group where you could join and you get free content about like how to sell more, how to get leads, how to build up your business. Right. When you follow me on Instagram, you will see them a lot. At the end of a post, I say, 'Hey, if you want to learn how to do That, just DM me the word so-and-so or just comment the word so-and-so.' So pretty much, then, after we got the leads, through these three types of strategies, we had to close the deals, right? So I'm a huge fan of call strategy, pretty much qualification call on the phone, and then closing call on Zoom. That's pretty much what we do on all our offers. Just because it's super simple, you get a lot of authority, especially if you have an appointment setter who can take the first call, and then they give you a lot of expert status. They talk about you like in such high words, like, 'Well, look, Nick has built this company for like five and a half years. He has spent so and so much money on ads. He's got so many jobs. Now, you can have pretty much his time in the call. I did the closing calls. You can have his time for free—for an hour, so better prepare all the questions you have. Then they came in and framed the correct way.' To answer your question, pricing was 8k when they paid in full. So, like, over the course of 10 months, that was the entire program, they could pay every month: 1,200 bucks. Does that make sense to everybody? Yeah. Cool. Does anybody else do that? And then back to zero but now back up again to 50k a month. And now it's much more sustainable and we'll be able to scale it to about 500k a month by the end of this year. And I still need three hours a week to deliver on it. That's it. Show you how. So we have a video course as base, pretty much like DMM. It's about 200 lessons in it. About how to scale your video business, like how to actually build your video business and then scale it up. That's what they watch on their own. Then we have a Facebook group. Pretty much as the DM had, where they have to share, like, they don't have to, but we do. We make them share wins. Okay, so whenever they close the deal, they pull Hashtag Win. And they get framed for that. Because we have an onboarding call as soon as somebody gets closed on the deal, they have an onboarding call. With my pretty much personal assistant. And he onboards them, and tells them, 'Look, guys. When you have a new deal, put that into the Facebook group because everybody Somebody else will come in and tell you, 'Hey, that's awesome.' Keep continuing. That's perfect. How did you do it? And then you guys can help each other out. So we make it a benefit for—if they post it into the group. And everybody does it all the time. Now, this is free content, right? Because when they post it, we screenshot it, upload it to the Instagram story, tag these people, and then we get additional reach because they repost the story. Okay. Then we have three weekly live calls. These are pretty much just Q&A calls. Needs no preparation from my side.' I just log in like three minutes before, hop on the call and tell Good Guys, what are your questions? What are your challenges? Where are you at right now? Now, in order to deliver killer results, like we don't just sit there and be like, 'Well, ask us your question.' Now, if they don't ask questions, which sometimes happens, like especially with a consulting offer in the beginning, you have, let's say, five people in it, you have six people in it, then 10, then 15. Sometimes it dries out. Nobody has questions anymore. So we call these people out. We're going to be like, 'Hey, Thomas, how's your business doing?' 'Hey, John, did you execute on what you promised last week?' 'Hey, whatever.' Just call them out and be like, 'Hold them accountable.' And that's like the absolute killer thing. We have a one-on-one WhatsApp support for absolute killer results. That means every single client has their own WhatsApp group with me and my team where they can ask us questions all the time and we can hold them accountable. Now, when we hold somebody accountable, that means when the live call was— so the weekly live call. Before the call starts, five minutes before, we send them a Short message, 'Hey, live call starts in five minutes, here's the link, join now.' Then after the live call, if Like my personal assistant, he writes down all the questions. He writes down the answers. And then after the call, he sends to everybody individually a message. Hey, these were your questions. These are the answers. Are you clear with that? is it clear to you what you need to execute on next okay so we hold them super accountable And then obviously soon as they post their first win into the Facebook group, we ask for referrals. So we just ask them, and that's an important thing. We call them and be like, hey. Awesome! Congrats on your first win. I have a question: Are you happy with the program so far? They always seem super hyped because they had just posted their first win. So what we do is: Who do you know specifically? That could benefit from the program as well. Important note here: We don't ask them, 'Do you know anybody?' We ask them, 'Who do you know specifically?' Because then they have to think. And when they say, 'Well, at the moment, I don't really know anybody,' we just keep digging. We say, 'Okay, but think about your family, your friends, who else is a videographer? Like, who else could benefit from that? You surely know some guy, right? Then we asked for a review and a video testimonial of these guys after the second win. Okay. So, notice after each win, we do something new. And then. We ask for an in-depth success interview after the fifth win, which is just pretty much a Zoom interview. Are you incentivizing them to give you referrals? Yes, we have like a referral program where they get 500 bucks on the first referral, then they get another 500 on the second, $ 500 on the third, and then on the fifth they get an iPhone 13. Always the latest iPhone and then every fifth is like another Perk and at some point, at the 20th, it's like a $20,000 red camera and that's what they're all aiming for pretty much. Cool. So it's like an in-depth success interview on Zoom that we can then use to promote it further. Any questions on that so far? How did you guys land on three people? That was pretty much the amount that the other guys had— like The Paoli Pros. And I just thought, okay, well, if they're doing it since like five years and it works so well, we'll just copy it. You could get away with less, I think. I've been in programs where they had like 17 weekly calls. These were all themed— like you had a sales weekly call, you had a marketing, you had a mindset, you had a technical check-in, you had like everything. But I much more prefer, especially in the beginning, to have less and then you can upgrade it later. Okay, so that led to... $127,402 in August 2021. Then we did, like, 100k in September. We did about 98k. In October, and then, as I said before, zero in November. Now, does anybody know or do you guys have an idea why we dropped to zero in November? This is a well-trained action. Euh, ja. You had it gone through your contacts list already and you had to do outcome. That was one thing, yeah. What else? didn't repeat successful actions. That's a good point. That's a good point. But mainly the problem was, as I said before, we worked with Service-based businesses in the beginning. So that means we had clients in the finance space, we had personal trainers, we had digital marketers, we had construction companies even, we had cleaning companies. We had marketing automation and process automation companies. So, like, so many different niches. And then I thought to myself, well, if we don't have a clear target audience, that means we have an unclear messaging in the ads, right? We have unclear targeting. And therefore, we have much higher cost per lead. And then I thought, 'Aha, that was the exact same problem that I ran into with the marketing agency, where, literally, in the beginning, we served 25 niches, and then, as soon as we niched down to car dealerships, we got way better results, we made more money, and everybody was happier. Even though, like, in the end, it didn't work out obviously, but I thought, 'Ah, Okay, cool, so I'm gonna do the same again. Let's just work with videographers only. And then we hit zero in November. The reason why is because we switched over from an offer to like 20 different niches, to an offer that only served one niche. And as soon as you do that and you haven't planted the seeds in that niche, you're starting from zero. There's no way that you can keep up that momentum if you just switch niches. He's just sitting there, sitting there with the arm up.' So you decided to switch after you hit zero? no no we switched and then we hit zero yeah that was just because we killed momentum and i didn't realize that we've built up a lot of momentum in that time to the videographers because i knew first of all i built a video agency so it was way easier to solve all their problems because i realized that the more clients we onboarded and we had about like 40 clients at that The more clients we onboarded, the more it got complicated. Exactly. Because they're sitting in these calls and now the Cleaning company asks a question. which is totally irrelevant to the fitness training. And then they start getting pissed. They don't come into calls anymore. And I knew, okay, we have to change. We have to switch it over to just like one niche. Yeah, only consulting. So you can only upgrade into, like, a bigger package. But at that time, I said, like, I was thinking, like, what the fuck? I didn't realize what the problems were. But another big problem— why we went down to zero and couldn't keep up the momentum— was this one. And that's actually a lesson important for everybody in here. I think the the last days, like today and yesterday, I heard so many people getting amped up about trading crypto NFTs. So many things we can do because we all have skills. Right, you guys are all ballers— you have skills that make a lot of money. But the problem is, and I I can't remember who it was, um, I think Nick Mullin. Yesterday we talked about if you do two things at the same time, it's not actually two, it's half and half. All right. And if you do seven, obviously, the percentage drops and drops and drops. And that was the problem. I had the video agency, had the Marketing agency, had the consulting offer, had some was trading a little bit, had an apparel brand, had a mastermind. I did in Dubai and then so many different things that I could just couldn't focus. I don't know who of you guys follows Sam ovens content. You probably have seen that graphic before of the unfocused Versus the focused. Have you? Okay, cool. So pretty much the unfocused guy focuses his energy in one thing, a second thing, a third thing, a fourth thing, a fifth one, a sixth one, seventh, whatever. While the focused one, wait, there's the seventh. While the focused one. just focuses it all in one thing. Now, who's going to become the expert in that one thing? It's pretty obvious, right? So I thought, cool, let's just focus. And that's when I finally decided it was overdue to fucking sell that video agency, stop all the other things. We're not making a lot of money, so I'm gonna cancel this. I'm gonna cancel this gonna Cancel this now today. Obviously, I still have the video agency until it's sold, but I just do the consulting. I have so much fucking time. But obviously, I don't, because this forces me— like having more time— forces me to in my business that would actually accelerate it that would move me forward. And that's finally now. The moment where I have time to do it every single day. Right. And then another thing: somebody said it before. I can't remember. Was I tried new strategies all the time instead of sticking to what was working? I think surez said it. Like, we just started doing the one call clause because I've seen somebody else do the one call clause and it was pretty much shiny object syndrome again. So that is one of the key takeaways for you guys and I'm actually going to go through all the key takeaways from this presentation right now. But first of all, what are the key takeaways for you guys so far? Definitely repeating successful actions focusing on the area. Thank you. Did you end up... Yeah, exactly. That was actually when we put together that messaging for the videographers. After we niched down, we went to zero. Because we lost momentum. Yeah, so did you niche back out or did you stay committed? No, we stayed committed to the niche and we're now back at like 40, 50K. Yep. Yeah, exactly. Like the calls are way more interesting because, like, every single question is relevant to everybody on the call. All right. And then, also, like, my Instagram following is growing way faster. I know you guys, some of you have, like, thousands, hundreds of thousands of followers. I'm at, like, two. But I just started running ads like a month ago and I gained a thousand followers in that month for about 10 bucks, 15 bucks a day. Right and that was just because it was very clear messaging to these types of people. Because the ad goes pretty much like this: 'Hey, my name is Nick and this in the background. Here is my red camera that I bought when I was 20 years old. And if you want to learn how you can do the same, then just follow me on Instagram. Right? But if you don't have a niche down audience, like, how are you going to pull that off? It's not gonna happen. Just to follow me. So it's like an ad that gets into my Instagram. Thank you. Then they get all my content and then the content has call to actions. All right. And you're going to see it afterwards when you look at that framework. That I gave you. When you look at the framework that I gave you, you're going to see there are multiple triggers. Yeah, the inner circle pretty much like, because as soon as they're in, in the inner circle, I have, I have the inner circle, uh, since pretty much two and a half years, um, since I started doing, like, like I got into consulting by accident at first, and I had a total of seven clients in there. Since two and a half years, I still have them. Like, they don't drop out as soon as I have them. Like, look at you guys. Are you going to drop out of Jeremy's Inner Circle? Probably not, right? You guys are making too much money being in here. Question. Thank you. Yes, sir. Thank you. How many referrals do you need for you to give them that, what's the camera set? Yeah, 20. between referrals. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Yeah, just do the math. All right. Cool. So just to summarize this for the key takeaways, obviously do the math. Don't chase shiny objects. Is so much easier said than done. We're all prey to that. Shiny objects are a bitch. And then, obviously, focus on one single thing. I cannot stress this enough. As soon as you start focusing on one thing, you have so much more time to do strategic thinking instead of just working in the business all the time and solving problems. All right, guys, pretty much it. Thank you very much for listening. Gracias. Thank you. Thank you.
